definition: Personal needs that support lifestyle, including eating, mobility, dressing, toileting, and personal hygiene. Self-dependence is fostered by ensuring the environment provides supplies and assistance to support self-performance.
Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body.
Movement of a limb toward the midline of the body.
The act of walking or moving from place to place. CNAs provide verbal prompts to guide ambulation toward desired locations and use gait/transfer belts to assist safely.
The heartbeat heard at the apex of the heart using a stethoscope, located at the 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line.

The absence of disease-causing microorganisms. Medical asepsis (clean technique) reduces the number of pathogens and prevents their spread from one person or place to another. Surgical asepsis eliminates all microorganisms. 'Clean' means free of pathogenic organisms; 'dirty' means contaminated or soiled.
The inhalation of food, fluid, or foreign material into the airway, which can cause choking or pneumonia. Prevented by keeping the head of bed elevated at least 30-45 degrees during and after meals.

The force exerted by blood against the walls of the arteries. Recorded as systolic (contraction) over diastolic (relaxation) pressure. Normal adult range: less than 120/80 mmHg. Hypertension: 130/80 or higher. Hypotension: below 90/60. Use correct cuff size; place cuff 1 inch above the antecubital space.

The use of correct posture, balance, and movement to prevent injury when lifting, moving, or repositioning patients. Keep back straight, bend at knees, keep load close, avoid twisting, feet shoulder-width apart for wide base of support. Push/pull rather than lift when possible.
A flexible tube inserted into the body to drain fluids; a urinary (Foley) catheter drains urine from the bladder. Catheterized patients are at increased risk for urinary tract infections.
Permanent shortening and tightening of muscles or tendons causing deformity and limited range of motion, often resulting from prolonged immobility.

Also known as a pressure ulcer or bedsore; a wound caused by prolonged pressure on skin over bony prominences. Prevented by repositioning at least every 2 hours, keeping skin clean and dry, and not massaging reddened areas.
Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath. Report immediately; position patient in Fowler's position to ease breathing.
Swelling caused by excess fluid accumulation in body tissues. Weight change with swollen legs or feet is a warning sign to report (STOP and WATCH).
The ability to understand and share the feelings of another person; different from sympathy in that it involves perspective-taking. A key characteristic of an effective CNA.
Straightening of a joint, increasing the angle between bones.
Bending of a joint, decreasing the angle between bones.

A semi-sitting position where the head of the bed is raised 45-60 degrees. Semi-Fowler's is 30-45 degrees. High Fowler's is 90 degrees. Used to aid breathing and feeding. The best position during and after meals to prevent aspiration.

Also called a transfer belt; a belt worn by the resident so the nursing assistant can hold and support them during ambulation or transfers. Provides a secure grip around the patient's waist for safe movement.

The single most important method of preventing the spread of infection. Wash with soap and water for at least 20 seconds. Use alcohol-based sanitizer (at least 60% alcohol) when hands are not visibly soiled. Wash before/after patient contact, after removing gloves, before eating, after restroom use, before and after breaks, and before entering a clean supply room.
An emergency technique used to dislodge an object from the airway of a choking person using abdominal thrusts. Indicated when a resident has a blocked airway.
High blood pressure; defined as 130/80 mmHg or higher.
Low blood pressure; systolic reading below 90/60 mmHg. Orthostatic hypotension occurs when blood pressure drops upon standing.
The inability to control bladder (urinary) or bowel (fecal) function. Requires perineal care after each episode and use of moisture barriers to protect skin.
Measurement and recording of all fluids taken in (oral, IV) and all fluids expelled (urine, drainage) to monitor fluid balance. 8 oz = approximately 240 mL.

Practices used to prevent the spread of infection. Transmission-Based Precautions include: Airborne (N95 respirator, negative pressure room — TB, measles, chickenpox), Droplet (surgical mask — influenza, meningitis, mumps), and Contact (gown and gloves — MRSA, C. diff, gastrointestinal/skin/wound infections).
Side-lying position; used to relieve pressure on the back and sacrum.
A technique used to turn a patient as a unit, maintaining spinal alignment; used for patients with spinal injuries.
Failure to provide the standard of care that a reasonably prudent person would provide, resulting in harm to a patient.
Communication without words. How you feel about a person, assignment or task may be communicated nonverbally through: facial expressions, body language, posture, touch, and tone of voice.
Latin for 'nothing by mouth' (nil per os); a patient restriction from eating or drinking, often before surgery or procedures.
A surgically created opening (stoma) in the body to divert waste. A colostomy diverts stool; a urostomy diverts urine.

A microorganism (germ) that causes disease, including bacteria (TB, pneumonia, strep throat), viruses (influenza, common cold, herpes, HIV/AIDS), and fungi (athlete's foot, ringworm, yeast infections). A pathogen that spreads easily from person to person is contagious.

Equipment worn to minimize exposure to hazards, including gloves, goggles, face shields, masks, laboratory coats, gowns, shoe covers, and surgical caps. Donning order: Gown → Mask → Goggles/Face shield → Gloves. Removing order: Gloves → Gown → Mask.
Lying face down on the abdomen.

A non-invasive measurement of oxygen saturation in the blood (SpO2). Normal range: 95-100%. Below 90% is a medical emergency — report immediately. Poor circulation and nail polish can affect readings.
The full movement potential of a joint. Active ROM is performed by the patient; Passive ROM (PROM) is performed by the CNA. PROM stands for Passive Range of Motion.

The process of breathing; one respiration = one inhalation + one exhalation. Normal adult rate: 12-20 breaths/minute. Apnea = absence of breathing. Count respirations after taking pulse so patient is unaware.

Devices used to limit patient movement; require a physician order, patient/family consent, and regular monitoring. The least restrictive method must always be tried first. Check every 30 minutes; release and reposition at least every 2 hours. Never tie to side rails.

The procedures, actions, and processes a healthcare worker is permitted to perform based on their training, experience, and licensure. CNAs work under supervision of licensed nurses (RN/LPN) and must never perform tasks outside their scope of practice.
Lying flat on the back (face up).

A vital sign measuring body heat. Normal oral temperature: 98.6°F (37°C). Routes: Oral (most common), Rectal (most accurate, 1° higher), Axillary (armpit, 1° lower), Tympanic (ear), Temporal (forehead). Wait 10-20 minutes after the resident has had a cold or hot drink before taking oral temperature. Report above 100.4°F (fever).
See Gait Belt; worn by the resident so the nursing assistant can hold and support them. Used when assisting patients with walking or transferring.
Position where the body is laid flat on the back with the feet elevated higher than the head; used in some emergency situations.

An infection in any part of the urinary system. One of the most common healthcare-associated infections. Common in catheterized patients; symptoms include burning, frequency, and cloudy or dark urine. Prevented by proper perineal care (front to back for females) and good hand hygiene.

A biopsychosocial model that assists staff in planning care for residents. Supports remaining abilities and caring relationships. Each letter represents a level in the hierarchy of needs: Biological, Activities of Daily Living, Societal, Inter-personal, Creative, Symbolic.
Basic needs for: food, water, oxygen, safety, rest, and human stimulation. When met, fosters self-preservation — the resident is physically cared for and safe.

The need for unique personal identity, privacy, and the resident's place in society. When met, fosters self-identity through privacy, affiliation with groups, culture, family customs, education, and resident selection of associations.

The need for connection with others: love and belonging. When met, fosters self-esteem through social role expression, supports interpersonal and social abilities, promotes social confidence, and creates an atmosphere of caring.

The need for personal expression, problem solving opportunities, and meaningful activity that brings joy to the resident's life. When met, fosters self-expression through independent activity, humor, creativity, and use of talents and skills.

The need for expression of beliefs, hopes, dreams, values, and autonomy (ability to control important aspects of life). Includes sense of peace according to the resident's belief in the meaning of life. When met, fosters self-actualization through hopefulness and spiritual support.

The particular character, or aggregate of qualities, that distinguishes one person or thing from others; sole and personal nature. Proper care requires establishing a relationship based upon mutual respect and understanding of each resident as an individual human being with personal needs and expectations.

A genuine passion to be open and flexible with others; to accept differences and build on similarities; to be willing to learn from others as cultural informants. The CNA's commitment to care for all residents regardless of their cultural values, beliefs, customs, or practices.

An active process of listening not only to words but also to how they are expressed. Requires effort, skills, and practice. Components include: Encouraging, Clarifying, Restating, Reflecting, Summarizing, and Validating. Use your ears and eyes; people provide information about their feelings and emotions as well as conveying a message.

The oral or written exchange of information. Has 4 parts: the speaker or sender, the message being sent, the listener or receiver of the message, and feedback. Choose words that are positive, non-threatening, and understood by the resident.

A condition in which the resident is able to hear and, in most cases, understand you, but is unable to speak. The resident may try to answer but the words may not come, or they may use a word that does not fit. Allow nonverbal communication such as gestures, writing, pointing, or picture boards.

A relationship between an individual who suffers from a disability or discomfort (resident) and a practitioner (caregiver) who possesses a specific technical skill or knowledge and who occupies a recognized role. Goals: to promote communication of distressing thoughts/feelings, assist with problem solving for daily living, and promote self-care and independence.

A healthcare worker who serves as the eyes, ears, and hands of the licensed nurses. Responsible for basic day-to-day care of residents including: bathing, dressing, hair care, mouth care, nail care, shaving, toileting, vital signs and weight, food service, feeding, bed making, moving the resident, environmental cleanliness, and reporting observations.

A written account of a person's illness and response to treatment and care. It provides a way for the health team to communicate information about the person. The record is a permanent, legal document. Documentation types include: MDS, ADL Documentation, Care Plan, Meal Consumption Record, Toileting Schedule, Bowel Record, Turning and Repositioning Record.

A complex form completed upon admission and at various times during a resident's stay at the nursing home. Includes information about the care the resident requires and receives. The MDS is the universal assessment tool used in long-term care settings.

A written guide about the care a resident should receive. A communication tool used by staff to ensure the health team is giving the same care. Components include: Nursing Diagnosis, Goal, Measures or actions to achieve the goal, and Evaluation.

Interventions to Reduce Acute Care Transfers. A quality improvement program focusing on the management of acute change in resident condition. Includes clinical and educational tools and strategies for everyday practice in long-term care facilities. The Stop and Watch Early Warning Tool is used by CNAs to identify and communicate changes in residents.

An early warning tool used to identify changes in a resident's condition. STOP and WATCH acronym: Seems different than usual; Talks or communicates less; Overall needs more help; Pain - new or worsening/Participated less in activities; Ate less; No bowel movement in 3 days or diarrhea; Drank less; Weight change/swollen legs or feet; Agitated or nervous more than usual; Tired/weak/confused/drowsy; Change in skin color or condition; Help with walking/transferring/toileting more than usual.

Preserving the privacy of the residents in your care. All information related to residents is kept in strict confidence for use only by the care team. Breach of confidentiality is sharing information verbally or in written form with someone not on the care team or who does not have a release of information form. Confidential information includes: age, sex, race, religion, marital status, occupation, health information, SSN, insurance, lab results, MRI/CT scans, and any personal care.

Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act. Ensures the privacy of personal medical information shared with doctors, hospitals, and others who provide and pay for healthcare. Personal health information must remain confidential and cannot be shared without the individual's authorization. Applies to medical records on paper, in computers, and information communicated orally.

A tiny living organism that can be seen only through a microscope. Microorganisms are everywhere: in the air, on the objects we touch, and in our bodies. Pathogens are microorganisms that cause disease. Most pathogens grow best where there is warmth, moisture, darkness, a food source, and oxygen.

For an infection to occur, all 6 links must be present: (1) Infectious Agent — the pathogenic microorganism; (2) Reservoir — any place pathogens can live and multiply; (3) Portal of Exit — the opening a pathogen uses to leave the body; (4) Mode of Transmission — how infection is carried (contact, droplet, airborne, common vehicle, vector); (5) Portal of Entry — the point a pathogen enters the body; (6) Susceptible Host — a person whose defenses against infection are weakened. Breaking any link stops the infection.

Infections caused by bacteria resistant to many antibiotics. Examples include MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus) and VRE (Vancomycin Resistant Enterococcus). These infections do not respond to traditional antibiotic therapy. Require Contact Precautions (gown and gloves).

Disease-causing microorganisms present in blood or body fluids. Examples: Hepatitis B (HBV), Hepatitis C (HCV), and Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV). Transmitted through sexual contact, sharing needles, needle stick injuries, and contact with infected blood on non-intact skin or mucous membranes. NOT spread by kissing, hugging, sneezing, coughing, food/water, or casual contact.

A serious disease caused by a virus that attacks the liver. Can cause lifelong infection, cirrhosis (scarring), liver cancer, liver failure, and death. About 30% of persons have no symptoms. When symptoms occur: jaundice, fatigue, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, joint pain. Preventable by vaccination (3 injections: 2nd given 1 month after first, 3rd given 6 months after second). Vaccine available free to employees.

A serious disease caused by a virus that attacks the liver. Can lead to liver cancer, liver failure, or cirrhosis — irreversible and potentially fatal scarring of the liver. 80% of people have no symptoms; symptoms may not appear for 10-20 years. Symptoms: nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, loss of appetite, fatigue, pain over the liver, jaundice, dark-colored urine, pale stools.

Human Immunodeficiency Virus causes AIDS by infecting and damaging lymphocytes — white blood cells that fight infection. People may show no symptoms at time of infection; may take up to 10 years for symptoms to show. Symptoms: rapid weight loss, intense fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, persistent diarrhea, night sweats, pneumonia. Transmitted sexually, through needles, infected blood/organs, mother to infant.

The process of killing disease-causing microorganisms. Bleach is one commonly used disinfectant. Disinfection kills many, but not all, microorganisms. Cleaning (with soap/water or alcohol) is done first to remove large particles, followed by disinfection or sterilization.
The process of killing ALL microorganisms (both pathogens and non-pathogens) using very high temperatures with heat or steam under pressure. The machine used is called an autoclave.

A rigid container for needles, broken glass, safety razors, or other sharps used until filled to the line (about 3/4 full). The box is then locked, secured, and disposed of with biohazard/medical waste. Puncture resistant, labeled with the Biohazard symbol, color-coded in red, and leak-proof on sides and bottom.

Items contaminated with a resident's blood, body fluids, secretions, or excretions. Must be bagged in leak-proof plastic bags to remove from a resident's room. Regulated waste includes: liquid or semi-liquid blood, items contaminated with blood, items caked with blood, and contaminated sharps.
A device used to replace a missing part of the body.

The Occupational Safety and Health Administration is a federal government agency that makes rules to protect employees from hazards on the job. Mandates that employers have a written Exposure Plan to identify employees at risk and actions to take when an exposure incident occurs.

An infection acquired while in a healthcare setting. Most common types are urinary tract infections, surgical-site infections, pneumonia, or bloodstream infections. The single most important prevention measure is proper hand washing.

The respiratory disease caused by the virus SARS-CoV-2. Spreads primarily through person-to-person contact via respiratory particles (droplets and aerosols). Also capable of contact, droplet, and airborne transmission in healthcare settings. Symptoms include: fever or chills, new loss of taste/smell, cough, sore throat, shortness of breath, congestion, fatigue, nausea/vomiting, muscle aches, diarrhea, headache.

A person whose natural defenses against infection are weakened. A person's ability to resist infection relates to: age, nutritional status, stress, fatigue, medications, and presence of disease or injury. The elderly and those with diabetes, COPD, or renal disease are at higher risk.

Using the senses of sight, hearing, touch, and smell to collect information about a resident. Basic observations to report include: ability to respond, movement, pain or discomfort, skin condition, eyes/ears/nose/mouth, respirations, bowels and bladder, appetite, and ADLs.

Activities/Recreation Director: assesses and implements recreational needs. Administrator: planning/organizing/directing/supervising the facility. Dietician: assesses nutritional needs. LPN: provides direct care including medications under RN direction. Occupational Therapist: assists with ADL skills and adaptive equipment. Pharmacist: fills medication orders and monitors drug interactions. Physical Therapist: assists with musculoskeletal problems. Physician: diagnoses and treats diseases. RN: assesses, makes nursing diagnoses, plans and evaluates care. Social Worker: helps with social/emotional/environmental issues. Speech Therapist: treats speech, voice, communication, and swallowing disorders.

The overall well-being and life satisfaction of a resident in a care facility. Includes physical, mental, social, and spiritual dimensions. CNAs promote quality of life by honoring resident choices, maintaining dignity, supporting relationships, and providing person-centered care.

The degree to which healthcare services increase the likelihood of desired health outcomes and are consistent with professional standards. In nursing facilities, it encompasses clinical care, safety, dignity, and resident rights.

Care that respects and responds to individual resident preferences, needs, and values, ensuring they guide clinical decisions. Focuses on what matters most to the resident, not just clinical outcomes.

A care philosophy that addresses all dimensions of a person — physical, psychological, social, and spiritual. Recognizes that residents are more than their medical conditions. CNAs support the whole person by encouraging independence, honoring preferences, and supporting meaningful activities.

Federal and state mandated rights of nursing home residents, including: the right to dignity and respect, to be free from abuse and restraints, to privacy and confidentiality, to make choices about daily life, to participate in care planning, to voice grievances, to manage personal finances, and to have visitors.

Willful infliction of injury, unreasonable confinement, intimidation, or punishment that results in physical harm, pain, or mental anguish to a resident. Includes physical, mental/verbal, sexual, and financial abuse. ALL abuse must be reported immediately.

Any act that causes physical harm or injury to a resident. Examples include hitting, slapping, pinching, kicking, force-feeding, or improper use of physical or chemical restraints. CNAs must never participate in or witness physical abuse without reporting it.

Oral, written, or gestured language that includes threats, humiliation, harassment, or ridicule and causes a resident mental anguish or distress. Examples include yelling, swearing, threatening, or belittling a resident.

Non-consensual sexual contact of any kind with a resident, including touching, fondling, sexual coercion, or exposure. Also includes taking sexual photos of residents. Residents with dementia cannot legally consent to sexual activity.

Failure to provide goods and services necessary to avoid physical harm, mental anguish, or mental illness to a resident. Includes failure to provide adequate food, clothing, hygiene, medication, safety, or supervision. Both intentional and unintentional neglect must be reported.

The deliberate misplacement, exploitation, or wrongful use of a resident's personal belongings or money. Examples include stealing money, taking personal items, or using a resident's funds for personal gain.

Taking advantage of a resident for personal gain, including financial exploitation such as forging checks, coercing changes to a will, or misusing a power of attorney. CNAs must never accept money, gifts, or be named in a resident's will.
Separation of a resident from others or from their room against their will, or through coercion, as a means of punishment or convenience. This is a form of abuse and is prohibited.

An injury whose cause is not observed or is not consistent with the explanation provided. CNAs must document and report all such injuries immediately. The nursing home must investigate all injuries of unknown source.

A person required by law to report known or suspected abuse, neglect, or exploitation of vulnerable adults. All CNAs are mandatory reporters. Failure to report is a crime. Reports should be made to the supervisor and/or Adult Protective Services (APS).

Body system consisting of the heart and blood vessels that circulates blood throughout the body. The heart pumps oxygenated blood through arteries and receives deoxygenated blood through veins. Common disorders include hypertension, myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure, and angina.

A heart attack caused by blockage of a coronary artery, resulting in death of heart muscle tissue. Symptoms include crushing chest pain, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, and pain radiating to the left arm or jaw. A medical emergency — call 911 immediately.

A condition in which the heart is unable to pump enough blood to meet the body's needs, causing fluid to back up in the lungs and body tissues. Symptoms include shortness of breath, edema (especially in the legs/ankles), fatigue, and rapid weight gain.

Chest pain or pressure caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, typically triggered by physical activity or stress. Unlike a heart attack, angina is temporary and does not cause permanent heart damage. Managed with rest and nitroglycerin.

A condition in which narrowed blood vessels reduce blood flow to the extremities. Symptoms include pain, cramping, or weakness in the legs with activity (claudication), cold or discolored skin, and slow-healing wounds. Serious risk of amputation if untreated.

Body system that brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide. Consists of the nose, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, and alveoli (air sacs in the lungs). Common disorders include pneumonia, asthma, emphysema, influenza, and tuberculosis.

An infection of one or both lungs caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi, resulting in inflammation and fluid accumulation in the alveoli. Symptoms include fever, cough with colored mucus, shortness of breath, and chest pain. Common in elderly and immunocompromised residents.

A chronic respiratory condition characterized by episodes of airway inflammation, swelling, and bronchospasm that narrow the airways and make breathing difficult. Symptoms include wheezing, coughing, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. Triggered by allergens, exercise, or irritants.

A chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) in which the air sacs (alveoli) are damaged and enlarged, reducing the lungs' ability to exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide. Primarily caused by smoking. Symptoms include chronic shortness of breath and barrel-shaped chest.

A highly contagious viral respiratory illness (the flu) spread through respiratory droplets. Symptoms include sudden onset of fever, chills, headache, muscle aches, fatigue, and respiratory symptoms. Prevented by annual vaccination. Can be life-threatening in elderly residents.

A contagious bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs. Spread through airborne droplets. Symptoms include persistent cough (sometimes with blood), night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. Requires airborne precautions (negative pressure room, N95 respirator).

Administration of supplemental oxygen to residents with hypoxia or respiratory distress. Delivered via nasal cannula (1-6 L/min), simple face mask, or non-rebreather mask. CNAs must never adjust oxygen flow rates without a nurse's order. Oxygen is flammable — no smoking or open flames near oxygen.

A device used to deliver supplemental oxygen through two small prongs inserted into the nostrils. The most common oxygen delivery device, providing low-flow oxygen at 1-6 liters per minute. CNAs check that prongs are correctly positioned and that tubing is not kinked.

The pulse measured at the radial artery on the inner wrist (thumb side). Counted for 60 seconds (or 30 seconds x2 if regular). Normal adult pulse: 60-100 beats per minute. Report a rate below 60 or above 100, or an irregular rhythm, to the nurse immediately.

The pattern or regularity of heartbeats. A regular rhythm has equal intervals between beats. An irregular (arrhythmic) rhythm has uneven intervals and must be reported to the nurse. When an irregular pulse is detected, the apical pulse should be taken for a full 60 seconds.
